speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4, interview with Sonia Burstyn, December 10, 1996. So now you're in America. And you're working. And you're moving forward in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: To build your life. I went to school, like I mentioned before. And I finished the school. From that school, I started to go to offices. And I took up-- my favorite subject is bookkeeping. I was once a bookkeeper, a bookkeeper in charge, assistant to CPA. So I moved up and up. Then in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '40-- no in '54 or '55, I divorced my husband. I met another man. And living with that man, we decided that we want children. So we got the family bigger. I got two children, beautiful children, twins, a male and a female.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names? Their full names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marcy and Leonard. And they made our life sweet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Joseph Burstyn, B-U-R-S-T-Y-N. He was managing and owning-- he owned a taxi and limousine business. I was with the children, home. We bought a house. And we tried to forget the past and build a new future, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nothing unusual, except that my love-- my life was surrounded around the children and husband, like a housewife should. Didn't do too much, meaning that I worked once for my brother that had the telephone company. I did work for a few years, but then when the children were bigger. And then I stopped. In '90-- '90, my husband passed away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I retired, and now concentrating on my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bigger one is Michael, Michael Burstyn. He's seven years old now. The little one is a long name, Joseph Tumbarello, T-U-M-B-A-R-E-L-L-O. [LAUGHS] Now they are a part of my life, as a matter of fact, all my life. I help my son, and I help my daughter with the grandchildren, and making my life as sweet as I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of your life during the war, in terms of being a partisan, what do you think that taught you in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It taught me to-- not to give up. To sum it up-- not to give up. There were times that I was standing near the river and said, why do I fight? Who do I fight? The Germans, they'll never be conquered. I mean, they never will get out of here. My life is finished. And why should I suffer? But I go on. I fought. And I was very happy I joined the partisans, because I felt that I did something for the human race, that I fought what's coming to us. I fought for freedom, that people should not be oppressed and get back what's ours. So partisan gave me a lot of self-respect. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that led me to what I am now, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: And messages, now, as a grandmother for your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren, my children, fight for whatever you think is right and just. It's yours. Don't ever give up. I'm here today, alive, because I believed in it, to tell you this story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Burstyn, tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken before the war. A friend of ours from Ostrog came to visit us. And that was the picture. I didn't have it. But I've got it from my villagers, from Stadniki. On the left is my father, ready horseback riding.

speaker: interviewer
text: His full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abba Waldman. On the right-hand side is my mother, Gitel Waldman. It's their granddaughter, my niece, Ruth Waldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is she alive today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she is, in Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken before the war, also. And it was taken in Ostrog by a friend, also. And it is from the left, my oldest sister, Genia. Next to her is Mancia, the one that was killed with her two children and husband. Next to her is her housekeeper. On the bottom is Manya when she was a little girl, my sister, the youngest sister. Next to her is my killed sister's, Mancia, son, [NON-ENGLISH]. And next to him is my younger brother, Sami.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture was taken right before the war, 1941. And the background, the house, is Mogenitska's, the lady that owned practically the whole village. This picture is taken in Stadniki. And my two sisters are with me. In the back, that's me. In the middle is Genia, the oldest sister. And the third one is Manya, the baby sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of me and my first husband. It was done in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manny Mischa Steinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '43. No, sorry-- it was before we-- in the '50s--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I came, '46. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the synagogue of Ostrog. It's supposed to be the biggest synagogue in Poland. A beautiful decoration inside and outside was the top in history. It was in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today-- Russians used to have storage. But now people are trying to bring back and to decorate, to build it, and bring it back to the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when I went there, to Russia, to Ukraine, to visit my cousin that is still alive. And that was two years ago. Then it is-- today is-- '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in Stadniki from [NON-ENGLISH]. He was, and the whole family, employed by my father and mother. Next to him is his wife, Tanya. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This gentleman was all the time very close to us. He was the one to tell us of all the-- all the dangers that confronted us. He also made sure that where my father, my aunt, and the baby is buried in Stadniki nobody touches that place. Right now, it's untouchable. No cows, nobody goes there, because he makes sure that the Jews were buried there, and nobody is allowed to destroy it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that he's just one of the people that helped you in the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: You have a lot-- a whole list of them. And they have family members. There was Olga. There was Manya. There was Jacob. These people were working for us and lived near us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of them helped you survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of them. All of them. And there is a list of more, like Manya [NON-ENGLISH]-- I don't remember all of them. But, mainly, each one in their own way helped us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the picture is a memorial of the 3,000 Jews that were killed in the first [NON-ENGLISH], which was 1941. They built a beautiful road. And this memorial stands there. And they are respecting it very nicely. I have to tell, also, that Ostrog people were very nice, cooperative, because they helped in building and ideas. They all respected that place. And it's sacred place to them and to us, of course. I must mention, also, about Stadniki. A village like that, I don't think there is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there's no words to describe. Each one of them contributed to our living now. I have four-- I had four sister and brothers that lived through the war because of the Stadniki people. And when I came back now to visit them and see everything what's left to see, they were like brothers and sisters. And the warmth poured out of their hearts. So I had to thank them from the bottom of my heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I just wanted to clarify with the memorial that it is in memory of the Jews that were killed. And that's stated on the memorial itself, that the Jews of the area were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did mention it before, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And also to finish the issue about the town, you talked about the cemetery, tombstones, and what the Germans did with these tombstones during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The cemetery was a huge cemetery in Ostrog. And what the-- the Russians did, leveled off all these stones. And they built a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is where people used to come and dance. And they had planted the trees and made a very-- a place for people to go and have fun. There were only few stones that were left behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the dance floor, you have the upturned face of the tombstones written in Hebrew that make the floor of this dance floor in the middle of the town. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's something similar to that, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, my second husband, with me. And it was taken in New York, in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband died when?

speaker: interviewer
text: '95. And your husband's full name?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1990. So this is taken before 1990?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. It's '87, 1987. Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Burstyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you in the picture with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That's me with him together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the picture is the wedding of my daughter. On the left side is my daughter. I'm in the middle. And this is my son-in-law. And they got married in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nassau, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marcy Burstyn and Jack Tumbarello.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Leonard Burstyn. It was taken in Florida at the wedding in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Nassau two months ago. And I am with my two grandchildren that I love dearly. The little one is my daughter's son, Joseph Tumbarello. And the bigger one is my son's son, Michael Burstyn, my pride and joy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these were taken in 1996 here in New York, in Nassau County?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you very much.
